ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Paya Soup; mapped to closest parent 'meat soup' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor. Mutton Paya Soup is a slow-cooked goat/sheep trotter broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Meat soups and broths) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Mutton Paya Soup in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Mutton Paya Soup as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mutton Paya Soup is approximately 400 g, typically served as a main dish at breakfast or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Mutton Paya Soup are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using mutton/goat meat and bone broth composition data as the closest approved reference. Values reflect the slow-cooked trotter broth with spices. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with published ICMR-NIN data for mutton-based preparations. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (goat meat, cooked) for additional validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate meat broth
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Paya Soup. GI estimated at approximately 5 (low) based on analogy with low-carbohydrate meat broths and bone broths documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Carbohydrate content is approximately 3.75 g per 100 g, primarily from spices and trace vegetables; this negligible carbohydrate load makes the GI clinically irrelevant. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (5 × 15.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.75, which is very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 300 mg per 100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, which is a significant proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 ml and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 1200 mg sodium; the potassium (720 mg per bowl) and magnesium (100 mg per bowl) content partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.75 g per 100 g) and high protein (8.75 g per 100 g) result in a negligible glycemic index (~5) and glycemic load (~0.75 per serving), supporting stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Paya Soup is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index (~5), high protein content that promotes satiety, and minimal carbohydrate load; however, sodium intake should be monitored if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.0 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (25 mg per 100 g) are present; a 400 g bowl delivers 8 g saturated fat and 100 mg cholesterol, which is relevant for patients on lipid-lowering regimens.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Paya Soup in moderation, limiting to one small bowl (200–250 ml) and avoiding added ghee, as the saturated fat and cholesterol content can contribute to LDL elevation; the collagen-rich broth may support vascular integrity but does not offset the lipid concern at large portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in collagen peptides and gelatin released from slow-cooked trotters, which may support joint cartilage health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton Paya Soup is traditionally valued for joint health due to its high collagen and gelatin content from slow-cooked trotters; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit, but those with elevated uric acid or gout should avoid it due to high purine content.

gout.key_factor: Mutton trotters are high in purines, which are metabolised to uric acid and can precipitate gout flares.
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia should avoid Mutton Paya Soup, as the slow-cooked trotter broth concentrates purines and can significantly elevate serum uric acid levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Paya Soup is a traditional slow-cooked South Asian broth prepared from the trotters (feet) of goat or sheep, known as 'paya' in Urdu and Hindi. The dish is a cornerstone of Hyderabadi and Mughlai cuisine and is widely consumed across India and Pakistan. Preparation involves simmering the trotters for 4–8 hours (or pressure-cooking for 1–2 hours) with whole spices including cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, and bay leaves, along with ginger-garlic paste, onions, turmeric, and chilli. The prolonged cooking releases collagen, gelatin, and bone marrow into the broth, producing a rich, viscous, deeply savoury liquid with a golden-brown colour and a gelatinous texture when cooled. The flavour profile is intensely meaty, aromatic, and mildly spiced. Nutritionally, Mutton Paya Soup provides approximately 95 kcal per 100 g, with 8.75 g protein, 5.0 g fat, and only 3.75 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. It is a meaningful source of collagen-derived amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline), zinc (1.2 mg), phosphorus (120 mg), niacin (4.5 mg), and vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 5 (very low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is notable at 300 mg per 100 g, requiring portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is traditionally served as a Sunday breakfast in North India and Pakistan, often accompanied by naan or kulcha bread. It is also consumed as a restorative meal during recovery from illness or bone injuries. The dish is halal and gluten-free by default.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using mutton/goat meat and bone broth composition data as the closest approved reference. No exact entry for Mutton Paya Soup exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to low-carbohydrate meat broths. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for mutton-based preparations and bone broth; no exact Mutton Paya Soup entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values should be treated as best estimates pending direct laboratory analysis. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mutton Paya Soup (slow-cooked trotter broth) are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as 'vegetarian' in dietary_tags; this has been corrected to is_vegetarian: false as the dish is made from animal trotters. 4. Duplicate portions in the input (three identical '1 serving (300ml)' entries) have been deduplicated; the canonical serving size is set to 400 g (1 bowl) per the grabfood portion entry, which is the more commonly cited standard bowl size. 5. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; the glycemic load per 400 g serving is calculated as (5 × 15.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.75. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 (meat soup) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (Meat soups and broths) are used as closest_parent matches; no exact codes exist for Mutton Paya Soup. 7. This dish is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual requirements for Chinese, Malay, and Tamil do not apply. Hindi and Urdu names are provided as the dish originates from the Indian subcontinent. 8. The input source 'grabfood' has been removed as a primary nutrient evidence source per Project Veritas forbidden-source policy; grabfood values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat have been replaced with ICMR-NIN/USDA-adapted estimates. 9. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
